coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots-modulated covalent triazine frameworks with exceptionally rapid hydrogen peroxide production in water
coredata.dc:creator: Ren, Weijie
Chang, Qing
Li, Ning
Yang, Jinlong
Hu, Shengliang
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) are incorporated into the interlamination of covalent triazine frameworks (CTFs) for significantly enhanced photocatalytic H2O2 production. Benefiting from CDs-induced rapid nucleation and growth of CTFs on their surface, it can be realized to achieve 13.5-fold increase in hourly productivity and the unique nanoscale sandwich structures featuring excellent charge separation properties. The resultant photocatalyst allows both the oxygen reduction reaction and water oxidation reaction to generate H2O2 at the same time, exhibiting H2O2 yield exceeding the production rate of the pure CTFs 22.6 folds and a solar-to-chemical conversion efficiency of 0.16%. The experimental results reveal that CDs play multiple roles in CTFs, including timely extraction and storage of photoexcited holes and the creation of intermediate bands favoring long wavelength light absorption and utilization. Besides, CDs-induced surface polarization electric field in the interlamination of CTFs can be modulated by simply adsorbing alkali metal ions to further facilitate the carrier separation and migration. As thus, the promising H2O2 production efficiency is achieved.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Covalent triazine frameworks
Carbon dots
Photocatalysis
Hydrogen peroxide production
Charge separation efficiency

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Novel Materials for Energy and Advanced Applications

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effects of alkali metal ions on photocatalytic performance
Mechanism of photocatalytic H2O2 production and stability

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the formation processes of CTFs and CDs@CTFs.

Sample characterizations (a) FTIR spectra of CDs@CTFs, CTFs and DCB; (b) Raman spectra of CDs@CTFs and CTFs; (c) XRD patterns of CDs@CTFs and CTFs; (d) TEM image of CDs@CTFs; (e) HRTEM images of CDs@CTFs; (f) STEM-EDS mapping of CDs@CTFs.

Photocatalytic performance (a) Comparison of H2O2 production of CTFs and CDs@CTFs before and after adding hole sacrifice agent of ethanol under simulated solar irradiation; (b) Comparison of H2O2 production among CDs@CTFs with different amounts of CDs in the presence of ethanol; (c) Photocatalytic H2O2 production in the presence of CTFs, CDs/CTFs and CDs@CTFs; (d) Transient photocurrent responses of CTFs, CDs/CTFs and CDs@CTFs under illumination; (e) PL spectra of CTFs, CDs/CTFs and CDs@CTFs; (f) TFS results of CTFs, CDs/CTFs and CDs@CTFs; (g) UV–vis absorption spectra of CTFs, CDs/CTFs and CDs@CTFs; (h) H2O2 production of CDs@CTFs under 808 nm light irradiation; (i) Transient photocurrent responses of CTFs and CDs@CTFs under 808 nm light irradiation.

Effects of metal ions on photocatalytic performance (a) Comparison for photocatalytic H2O2 production of CTFs, CDs/CTFs and CDs@CTFs in Cs+ ions (0.5 M) solution; (b) Comparison for photocatalytic H2O2 production of CDs@CTFs in pure water and Cs+ ions (0.5 M) solution; (c-f) Photocatalytic H2O2 production (c), zeta potentials (d), transient photocurrent responses (e) and PL spectra (f) of CDs@CTFs in different solution of Cs+, K+, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+ with the same concentration of 0.05 M.

Photocatalytic mechanism and stability (a) Schematic illustration of photocatalytic mechanism of CTFs and CDs@CTFs; (b and c) H2O2 yield variations for CTFs and CDs@CTFs adding h+ (C2H5OH) and e- (NaIO3) scavengers; (d) Surface voltage variations of CDs@CTFs in the solution containing different alkali metal ions; (e) The cycling stability and durability of CDs@CTFs in photocatalytic activity.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots-modulated covalent triazine frameworks with exceptionally rapid hydrogen peroxide production in water

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Group of New Energy Materials and Devices, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Group of New Energy Materials and Devices

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Group of New Energy Materials and Devices, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) are incorporated into the interlamination of covalent triazine frameworks (CTFs) for significantly enhanced photocatalytic H2O2 production. Benefiting from CDs-induced rapid nucleation and growth of CTFs on their surface, it can be realized to achieve 13.5-fold increase in hourly productivity and the unique nanoscale sandwich structures featuring excellent charge separation properties. The resultant photocatalyst allows both the oxygen reduction reaction and water oxidation reaction to generate H2O2 at the same time, exhibiting H2O2 yield exceeding the production rate of the pure CTFs 22.6 folds and a solar-to-chemical conversion efficiency of 0.16%. The experimental results reveal that CDs play multiple roles in CTFs, including timely extraction and storage of photoexcited holes and the creation of intermediate bands favoring long wavelength light absorption and utilization. Besides, CDs-induced surface polarization electric field in the interlamination of CTFs can be modulated by simply adsorbing alkali metal ions to further facilitate the carrier separation and migration. As thus, the promising H2O2 production efficiency is achieved.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Covalent triazine frameworks (CTFs) have attracted considerable interest on account of their unique structures and good stability [1,2]. These features make CTFs useful for various applications in photocatalysis, gas/molecular adsorption and separation, energy storage and conversion and others [1–5]. Previous works have demonstrated that crystalline CTFs with well-ordered molecular and porous structures is highly beneficial to charge separation and transfer, which is a process whereby photoexcited electrons and holes arrive at the reaction interface, mostly deciding photocatalytic activity [6–8]. Nevertheless, obtaining crystalline CTFs is still extremely difficult due to the poor reversibility of the conjugated bond, which is not conducive to the self-adjustment [9]. Presently most of the reported CTFs exhibited low crystallinity or amorphous structure, thereby limiting their photocatalytic performance. To improve separation and transfer of charge carriers on CTFs, another feasible strategy is to introduce electron-donating and -accepting units with different electron affinities and ionization potentials into the skeleton of CTFs [5,10,11]. By incorporating acetylene or diacetylene moieties into polymer networks, for instance, photocatalytic H2O2 production of CTFs is dramatically promoted by suppressing charge recombination [12]. Similarly, Huang et al. [13] also reported that H2 evolution rate of CTFs was significantly enhanced after the incorporation of benzothiadiazole and thiophene moieties. However, this strategy generally suffers from costly reactive monomers as well as inefficient, stringent or intricate preparation steps.

Carbon dots (CDs) with ultra-small size (<10 nm) consist of crystalline graphitic cores surrounding with the surface terminal polar groups, which bridge the gap between the molecular and the nanoscale world due to their unique molecular-like behavior [14]. Therefore, CDs are easily incorporated into organic and inorganic semiconductor nanomaterials to serve as charge donor/acceptor and reservoir, facilitating charge transfer and suppressing the recombination of photocarriers [15–17]. Moreover, CDs possess many advantages regarding low cost, high stability, friendly environment and tunable optical properties [18–20]. In light of these considerations, numerous CDs-related hybrid architectures have been developed for the implementation of various solar energy conversions [14,21–25]. Although Chen et al. have prepared CDs/CTFs through an impregnation approach for photocatalytic H2 evolution [26], unfortunately in situ incorporating CDs into CTFs for the photosynthesis of H2O2 is not reported until now.

Since studying isolated components is no longer sufficient to solve the technological challenge of solar-to-chemical conversion (SCC), here CDs are employed as spacers between the layers of CTFs to enhance the photogenerated charge separation and transfer of CTFs. We demonstrate that CDs enable fast formation of CTFs on their surface with 13.5-fold increase in productivity, significantly reducing production cost of materials. On the other hand, CDs embedded into the layers of CTFs can modulate the electronic structures and rapidly capture photoinduced holes, not only efficiently impeding the recombination of charge carriers but also extending the utilization of long wavelength lights. This endows the hybrid product (CDs@CTFs) with superior photocatalytic activity toward H2O2 generation via an efficient dual-channel reaction pathway, i.e·H2O2 is produced by both the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR, Equation (1–3)) and water oxidation reaction (WOR, Equation (4–6)) simultaneously [27–29]:
(1)
O 2
+ 2
H
+
+ 2
e
-
→
H
2
O 2
(2)
O 2
+
e -
→ ·
O 2 -
(3)
O 2
-
+ 2
H
+
+
e
-
→
H
2
O 2
(4)
2
H 2
O
+ 2
h
+
→
H
2
O
2
+ 2
H
+
(5)
H 2
O
+
h +
→ · O H
+
H +
(6)
· O H +
· O H
→
H 2
O 2

As a result, the H2O2 production rate of CDs@CTFs is 22.6 times higher than that of pure CTFs in the absence of any sacrificial agents and its SCC efficiency reaches 0.16 %, surpassing that of the acetylene/diacetylene-functionalized CTFs reported previously. Besides, alkali metal ions in water allow CDs to rapidly extract holes from CTFs, favorably oxidizing water and enhancing the H2O2 production rates. As thus, CDs@CTFs could be utilized for the photosynthesis of H2O2 in seawater without incurring the burden of fresh water resource shortage.

All chemicals such as H2O2 (30 v/v%), formic acid (88 v/v%), Trifluoromethanesulfonic acid, 1,4-dicyanobenzene (DCB), dichloromethane, Dimethyl Formamide (DMF), NaIO3, Na2SO4, MgCl2, CaCl2, NaCl, KCl, CsCl were purchased from Aladdin, and the chemical reagents were utilized without further treatment. All aqueous solutions in the experiment were prepared using ultrapure water.

Absorption spectra were performed on a Shimadzu UV-2550 UV–vis spectrometer. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed on a FEI Tecnai G2 F20 microscope with a field-emission gun operating at 200 kV. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was recorded with a Bruker Vertex 70 spectrometer from 4000 to 500 cm−1. XPS data of all samples were collected using a Kratos AXIS 165 mutitechnique electron spectrometer with an Al Ka X-ray source for determining the composition and chemical bonding configurations. Fluorescence spectra were recorded with a Hitachi F4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer. Time-resolved PL lifetime measurements were conducted on Hamamatsu C11367 fluorescence spectrophotometer.

The CDs were prepared by our previously reported method [30]. Typically, 200 mg coal pitch powder was mixed with 30 mL formic acid and 3 mL H2O2 (30 wt%) in the beaker and stirred for 18 h. Subsequently, the precipitate was separated with the supernatant through the centrifugation of 10000 rpm, and the supernatant was put into the rotary evaporator to get the solid precipitate. Then the resultant precipitate was washed with ethanol and the resultant suspension was dialyzed in water for 2 days. After vacuum drying at 60 °C for 3 h, the dark brown CDs powder was collected.

CTFs was prepared in a mixture solution at the interface of dichloromethane and trifluoromethanesulfonic acid [31]. Typically, 1 mL of CF3SO3H was added into a 100 mL pre-dried flask and 200 mg 1, 4-dicyanobenzene (DCB) was previously dissolved into 20 mL CH2Cl2, respectively. The monomer solution was ultrasonic for 10 min in order to fully dissolve. Afterwards, the monomer solution is slowly added to the flask at 95 °C. Under the stirring, the interface was formed between CH2Cl2 and CF3SO3H because of the immiscibility of the two solvents, triggering the 2D polymerization by the cyclotrimerization reaction. The reaction system was equipped with a reflux unit and kept 95 °C heating and stirring 12 h to promote the complete reaction conversion for the formation of CTFs. The final product was obtained after washing with dichloromethane, ethanol and N, N-dimethylformamide for several times and drying in a vacuum oven at 80 °C for 5 h.

5 mg CDs was dispersed in 5 mL ethanol by treating with ultrasonic. Similarly, 100 mg CTFs was dispersed in 20 mL ethanol by stirring for 30 min and treating with ultrasonic for 30 min. Subsequently, the suspension of CDs was slowly added to CTFs solution under stirring intensively and then the resultant mixture was treated by ultrasonic for one hour. After drying in a vacuum oven at 80 ℃, the sample of CDs/CTFs was obtained finally.

Typically, 1 mL of CF3SO3H was added into a 100 mL pre-dried flask and 200 mg of 1, 4-dicyanobenzene and 10 mg CDs were previously dissolved into 20 mL CH2Cl2, respectively. The monomer solution was ultrasonic for 10 min in order to fully dissolve. Afterwards, the monomer solution is slowly added to the flask at 95 °C. Under the stirring, the interface was formed between CH2Cl2 and CF3SO3H due to the immiscibility of the two solvents, triggering the 2D polymerization by the cyclotrimerization reaction. The reaction system was equipped with a reflux unit and kept heating at 95 °C and stirring for 3 h to promote the complete reaction for the formation of CTFs. The final product was obtained after washing with dichloromethane, ethanol and N, N-dimethylformamide for several times and drying in a vacuum oven at 80 °C for 5 h.

The electrochemical measurement was conducted in the solution system of 0.1 M Na2SO4 with the three electrode unit. The working electrode, counter electrode, and reference electrode were the sample-coated ITO glass, the Ag/AgCl standard electrode, and the Pt plate, respectively. The electrochemical data was tested and recorded by the SP-200 Bio-logic electrochemical workstation. The photocurrent test was conducted in the three-electrodes device with 0.1 M Na2SO4 as the electrolyte. The working electrode, counter electrode, and reference electrode were the sample-coated ITO glass, the Ag/AgCl standard electrode, and the Pt plate, respectively. The system was put in a dark box and Xenon light was placed out of the box and the light can shine into it through the hole. The I-t curves were tested and recorded by the SP-200 Bio-logic electrochemical workstation.

Typically, 30 mg catalyst and 30 mL water were put in a 50 mL beaker. Prior to the photocatalytic tests, the suspension was stirred for 30 min in dark to reach the absorption–desorption equilibrium. The whole reaction was carried out under simulated sunlight (light intensity: 100 mWcm−2). After a certain period, the solution was collected and the absorbance was tested with the UV–visible spectrometer. For example, A certain amount of the reacted solution was extracted and then filtered with a 0.22 μm filter to remove the photocatalyst. Subsequently, 1 mL of the resultant solution sample was mixed with pre-prepared Ce(SO4)2 solution and the concentrations of H2O2 was determined by the UV–visible spectrometer.

CDs@CTFs were prepared by catalytic trimerization reaction of 1,4-dicyanobenzene (DCB) at the interface of CH2Cl2 and CF3SO3H in the presence of CDs. In general, the cyclotrimerization reaction of DCB is sluggish under no excessive CF3SO3H and the overall reaction usually takes very long time, unfavorable scale production. As illustrated in Fig. 1 , CDs as heterogeneous nucleation agents of CTFs can remarkably facilitate the formation of CTFs in the reaction system. The cyclotrimerization reaction of DCB can be almost completed in 3 h in the presence of CDs while it needs 12 h to complete in the absence of CDs under the same conditions (Figure S2). The hourly productivity of CTFs enhances by 13.5-fold in the presence of CDs based on our calculation (see Experimental section in Supplemental Information). To clarify why CDs allow CTFs to nucleate on their surface, zeta potentials of DCB, CDs and their mixture in CH2Cl2 were tested under the same conditions and the obtained results revealed the strong interaction between CDs and DCB (Figure S3). Thus, CDs could be easily involved in the cyclotrimerization reaction of DCB and be forced to enter into the layers of CTFs during the growth process, bringing a novel hybrid heterostructure. It is evident that as-prepared powder of CDs@CTFs displays much deeper color than that of pure CTFs by the naked eye observation (Figure S4).

Fourier transformed infrared (FTIR) spectra (Fig. 2 a) of DCB, pure CTFs and CDs@CTFs show strong peaks at 1353, 1500 and 1575 cm−1 indicating the formation of triazine rings after trimerization, while the stretching peak at 2230 cm−1 derived from the carbonitrile groups of the monomers almost disappears in their spectra [31–33]. Raman spectra (Fig. 2b) of CTFs and CDs@CTFs display graphene-like sharp peaks, which are ascribed to a two-dimensional honeycomb structure within CTFs [32]. The crystalline structures of CTFs and CDs@CTFs were further investigated by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern. From Fig. 2c, both XRD patterns feature a same stacking mode, but the diffraction peak at around 18° and 24° of CDs@CTFs exhibits a slight shift compared to that of pure CTFs, suggesting that the stacking structure of the layered CTFs is intervened by CDs. Additionally, CDs@CTFs also reveal the higher Brunauer − Emmett − Teller (BET) surface area and more kinds of pore sizes than pure CTFs on the basis of the characterization of nitrogen adsorption − desorption isotherms (Figure S5). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images show that the large size of ultrathin CTF nanosheets is ornamented with numerous small nanoparticles (Fig. 2d). By further high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) observation, it can be found that CDs are embedded in the layers of CTFs or wrapped by the polymer of CTFs (Fig. 2e). The STEM-EDS mapping further verifies that CDs are evenly dispersed all over CTFs since O element only stems from CDs (Fig. 2f).

Fig. 3 a reveals the photocatalytic activity of pure CTFs and CDs@CTFs before and after adding hole sacrifice agent of ethanol under simulated solar irradiation. CDs@CTFs produces H2O2 much higher than that of pristine CTFs, presenting 22.6-fold increase in production rate. Compared with other metal-free photocatalysts reported previously (Table S1), our sample of CDs@CTFs shows more rapid H2O2 mass evolution rate. Meanwhile, this hybrid product is demonstrated with an apparent quantum yield (AQY) of 0.93 % at 505 nm and a SCC efficiency of 0.16 %, which is higher than that of the acetylene/diacetylene-modified CTFs [34–36]. After adding ethanol to weaken the surface recombination of photogenerated electron and hole and furnish H+ (CH3CH2OH + 2 h+ → CH3CHO + 2H+)25, the production rate of H2O2 from both samples increases relatively. Fig. 3b shows the photosynthetic rate of H2O2 upon CDs@CTFs with different amounts of CDs to assess the added optimum mass of CDs. It can be found that the photocatalytic activity of CDs@CTFs first increases and then decreases with the content of CDs. Adding 5 wt% CDs in the preparation system endows CDs@CTFs with the highest photosynthetic rate of H2O2 in the presence of ethanol. Notably, we verified the inert ability of CDs@CTFs to decompose H2O2 by adding them into the H2O2 solution (Figure S6), implying that CDs@CTFs benefit for high-concentration H2O2 production. To compare, we further tested the photocatalytic activity of a contrast sample (named as CDs/CTFs) obtained by mixing CDs and CTFs under sonication. Under the same conditions, the H2O2 production rate of CDs/CTFs is clearly lower than that of CDs@CTFs, implying different structures between CDs@CTFs and CDs/CTFs (Fig. 3c).

To uncover the roles of CDs in CDs@CTFs, we conducted the test of the photocurrent responses, which is associated with the separation and transfer efficiency of photoexcited electrons and holes. From Fig. 3d, the photocurrent density of CDs@CTFs is significantly higher than that of CDs/CTFs and CTFs, revealing the CDs@CTFs have more superior charge separation properties and more usable surface carriers in photosynthetic reaction [37]. This result is also supported by their photoluminescence (PL) spectra. Fig. 3e clearly shows that the PL intensity of the CDs@CTFs is lower than that of CDs/CTFs and CTFs. The lower PL intensity indicates that the incorporation of CDs into CTFs can suppress the recombination of photocarriers [38,39]. Furthermore, both transient fluorescence spectroscopy (TFS) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) are employed to analyze the motion characteristics of carriers in photocatalysts. Fig. 3f and Table S2 depicts that the average fluorescence lifetime of CDs@CTFs (8.27 ns) is longer than that of CDs/CTFs (7.54 ns) and CTFs (6.89 ns), indicating that that photoexcited electrons have more time to be transported to the catalyst surface [40,41]. Moreover, CDs@CTFs has a smaller charge transfer resistance than CDs/CTFs and CTFs (Figure S7), thus giving rise to faster interfacial electron transfer rates [42,43]. All of these properties enable CDs@CTFs to furnish more carriers for chemical reactions at a given number of photons. However, this should be not the whole story for the role of CDs in enhancing H2O2 generation of CTFs.

To determine the range of light utilization, the UV–Visible diffuse reflectance spectra of as-prepared powders of CTFs, CDs/CTFs and CDs@CTFs are provided in Fig. 3g. The CTFs mainly exhibit strong absorption in UV regions whereas the incorporation of CDs into CTFs extends light absorption to near infrared region. Previous works have proved that the fluorescence emission of CDs is popularly attributed to surface-related defective sites, which are easily influenced by surface modification [18,19]. Accordingly, in-situ nucleation of CTFs on the surface of CDs may create localized electronic states at their interface, resulting in new electronic transitions in CDs@CTFs. In order to examine the contribution of longer wavelength light on photosynthetic H2O2, the laser source with a wavelength of 808 nm was used for irradiating the suspension containing photocatalysts. As expected, CDs@CTFs realizes H2O2 generation under 808 nm light irradiation (Fig. 3h), whereas there is nearly no detectable product for the CTFs. Different from pure CTFs, CDs@CTFs distinctly present an observable photocurrent density under the same light illumination (Fig. 3i), agreeing well with the photocatalytic results in Fig. 3h. Consequently, it can be affirmed that the incorporation of CDs into CTFs extends the utilization of long wavelengths in photocatalytic H2O2 production.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of alkali metal ions on photocatalytic performance
ce:section.ce:para: Another interesting finding is that CDs can take advantage of alkali metal ions in water to boost H2O2 production (Fig. 4 a), inferring that their interaction induces polarization electric field with the ability to facilitate charge separation [44]. The controlled experiment of pure CTFs is unable to improve H2O2 yield by virtue of alkali metal ions. As Cs+ ions in the optimum concentration of 0.5 M (Figure S8) are added in photocatalytic reaction system, Fig. 4b illustrates that the H2O2 production rate of CDs@CTFs enhances by 58 % whereas that of CDs/CTFs presents a very limited increase (merely 7.8 %, Figure S9),which further certifies differences in structures of both samples. In addition, we examined the effects of different metal ions on H2O2 yield of CDs@CTFs (Fig. 4c). Under the same concentration condition, the contribution order is Cs+> K+ > Na+ > Mg2+> Ca2+ for activity. Moreover, the H2O2 yield of CDs@CTFs can be enhanced in the presence of Cs+ ions under 808 nm light irradiation (Figure S10), implying no new photoactive center formation after adding Cs+ ions.

Fig. 4d displays the effects of different metal ions on zeta potential of CDs@CTFs, which is well consistent with above the contribution order. Notably, the drastic reduction of zeta potential absolute value means that photocatalyst suspension becomes unstable. Just as observed in the experiment, the chelation of Ca2+ and Mg2+ ions induces the flocculation of suspended CDs@CTFs in water, thereby causing deterioration of their photocatalytic performance. To gain insights into the contribution of metal ions on photocatalytic activity, we further carried out photocurrent response analysis. From Fig. 4e, the photocurrent density of CDs@CTFs varies with metal ions, according with the order of Cs+> K+ > Na+ > Mg2+> Ca2+. This case is also verified by PL results (Fig. 4f and Figure S11). Accordingly alkali metal ions in solution largely benefit separation and transfer of photoinduced charge in CDs@CTFs [45].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of photocatalytic H2O2 production and stability
ce:section.ce:para: To unveil the mechanism of photocatalytic H2O2 production via CDs@CTFs, we investigated the electronic band structures of CTFs and CDs on the basis of the optical band gaps and the valence band (VB) positions determined using valence-band X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) (Figure S12). As illustrated in Fig. 5 a, the integration of CDs and CTFs provides suitable band edges to implement ORR (Equation (1–3)) and WOR (Equation (4–6)) at the same time [46,47]. The unique structure created by in situ nucleation of CTFs on the surface of CDs endows an ideal localized interface state for CDs@CTFs, which not only creates new energy levels for efficient utilization of visible and NIR light, but also promotes charge separation and transfer significantly. Without the aid of CDs, CTFs only enable two-electron ORR for H2O2 generation though their VB positions are also allowed to conduct two-electron WOR. This could be attributed to low crystallinity of CTFs, which easily incurs fast exciton recombination. CDs have unique hole-accepting property. When they are embedded into the layers of CTFs, CDs behave as hole acceptors [48] and prevent electron-hole recombination in CDs@CTFs, thus prolonging the lifetime of electrons in CTFs as shown in Fig. 3f. This feature not only favors the two-electron ORR process, but also probably allows WOR to involve in H2O2 production. To confirm it, we performed the control experiments. Fig. 5b and c presents that ethanol as hole sacrifice agent can enhance the H2O2 production of CTFs and CDs@CTFs under atmosphere. Most notably, no H2O2 is detected for pure CTFs under N2-saturated atmosphere using NaIO3 as electron sacrificial agent (Fig. 5b), but the ability of H2O2 production for CDs@CTFs keeps almost unchanged under the same catalytic conditions (Fig. 5c) [12]. Furthermore, the linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) was performed to determine the number of transferred electrons (n) for the ORR process based on the method reported by Feng et al. [49–51]. From Figure S13, the n value is obtained as 1.96, suggesting that two-electron oxygen reduction takes place over CDs@CTFs rather than one-electron oxygen reduction. Gas chromatography measurements further display no O2 production (Figure S14), thereby the other water oxidation process i.e·H2O + 4 h+ → O2 + 4H+ was inhibited upon CDs@CTFs. All of above experimental results demonstrate the incorporation of CDs into CTFs can simultaneously implement ORR and WOR to generate H2O2, distinguishing with the photocatalytic mechanism of CTFs alone [52].

Thanks to the band bending effect near the surface, a polarization electric field could be elicited in CDs@CTFs, impacting the rates of electron/hole spatial separation and transfer. The surface states of CDs could induce upward band bending at their surface, thus the polarization electric field is able to accelerate the hole transport from the bulk to the surface, leading to the increase of hole-induced reaction as presented above [53]. Particularly, the surface states of CDs highly rely on their surface terminal polar groups including alcohols, carboxylic acids and amines, which control the interaction of CDs with the surrounding environment. Due to the interaction of reactive surface groups with metal ions in water, the surface states of CDs may be modulated, changing the upward band bending degree. To explore the existence of this case, we evaluated the dependence of polarization electric field intensity of CDs@CTFs with different metal ions using the open-circuit potential measurements [54]. As shown in Fig. 5d and Figure S15, the surface voltage variation order follows as the corresponding activity contributions of metal ions, i.e. Cs+ > K+ > Na+ > Mg2+ > Ca2+. Nevertheless, it should be mentioned that the unique structure of CTFs-confined CDs considerably reinforces the effect of metal ions on the formed electric field.

The reproducibility and stability of the CDs@CTFs for photocatalytic H2O2 production were estimated under simulated solar light irradiation. From Fig. 5e, no noticeable decrease is observed in the H2O2 production rate, reflecting that our presented photocatalysts are highly stable and can be reused. Moreover, the photocatalytic activity of CDs@CTFs after 200-day storage in the lab was retested. The sample still maintains the same activity as the freshly prepared one (Fig. 5e). Meanwhile, FTIR and XRD results (Figure S16) certify the structure stability of CDs@CTFs as well.

In summary, we fabricated a novel metal-free hybrid photocatalyst by incorporating the low-cost and multifunctional CDs into the interlamination of CTFs. As heterogeneous nucleating agents, the CDs not only promote the nucleation and growth of CTFs enabling them 13.5-fold increase in hourly productivity, but also create unique hybrid nanoscale sandwich structure featuring highly efficient charge separation property. The obtained CDs@CTFs shows superior photocatalytic activity to produce H2O2 with 22.6 fold higher production rate than the pure CTFs in the absence of any sacrificial agents. Meanwhile, its SCC efficiency reaches 0.16 %. Such excellent photosynthetic H2O2 performance arises from the contributions of CDs in hole extraction and storage as well as surface energy traps, providing intermediate bands of long wavelength light utilization, suppressing electron-hole recombination and allowing ORR and WOR dual channels to generate H2O2 simultaneously. Moreover, we demonstrated that alkali metal ions in water can favorably oxidize water and enhance H2O2 yield through mediating surface polarization electric field of CDs embedded into CTFs. Therefore, the CDs@CTFs could be utilized for the photosynthesis of H2O2 in seawater without incurring the burden of fresh water resource shortage and material high cost.

Weijie Ren: Data curation, Methodology, Investigation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Qing Chang: Resources, Investigation, Software. Ning Li: Resources, Investigation, Validation. Jinlong Yang: Validation, Writing – review & editing. Shengliang Hu: Conceptualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Resources, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank for financial support Fundamental Research Program of Shanxi Province of China (20210302123037), Research Project Supported by Shanxi Scholarship Council of China (2022-136) and Specialized Research Fund for Sanjin Scholars Program of Shanxi Province of China.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2022.139035.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Covalent triazine frameworks as emerging heterogeneous photocatalysts

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Qian, Z.J. Wang, K.A.I. Zhang, Covalent Triazine Frameworks as Emerging Heterogeneous Photocatalysts, Chem. Mater., 33 (2021) 1909-1926.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Wang, H. Wang, Z. Wang, L. Tang, G. Zeng, P. Xu, M. Chen, T. Xiong, C. Zhou, X. Li, D. Huang, Y. Zhu, Z. Wang, J. Tang, Covalent organic framework photocatalysts: structures and applications, Chem. Soc. Rev., 49 (2020) 4135-4165.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Covalent triazine frameworks: synthesis and applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Liu, L. Guo, S. Jin, B. Tan, Covalent triazine frameworks: synthesis and applications, J. Mater. Chem. A 7(2019) 5153-5172.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Efficiently selective oxidation of H2S to elemental sulfur over covalent triazine framework catalysts

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W.L. Peng, X. Kan, W. Chen, J. Mi, G. Zhang, Y. Xiao, W. Liu, F. Liu, A. Zheng, Efficiently Selective Oxidation of H2S to Elemental Sulfur over Covalent Triazine Framework Catalysts, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces, 13 (2021) 34124-34133.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Unveiling charge dynamics in acetylene-bridged donor−π–Acceptor covalent triazine framework for enhanced photoredox catalysis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Lan, X. Liu, Y. Zhang, Q. Li, J. Wang, Q. Zhang, G. Bai, Unveiling Charge Dynamics in Acetylene-Bridged Donor−π–Acceptor Covalent Triazine Framework for Enhanced Photoredox Catalysis, ACS Catal., 11 (2021) 7429-7441.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Strong-base-assisted synthesis of a crystalline covalent triazine framework with high hydrophilicity via benzylamine monomer for photocatalytic water splitting

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Zhang, G. Cheng, L. Guo, N. Wang, B. Tan, S. Jin, Strong-Base-Assisted Synthesis of a Crystalline Covalent Triazine Framework with High Hydrophilicity via Benzylamine Monomer for Photocatalytic Water Splitting, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed., 59 (2020) 6007-6014.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Controlling monomer feeding rate to achieve highly crystalline covalent triazine frameworks

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Liu, K. Jiang, X. Ding, S. Wang, C. Zhang, J. Liu, Z. Zhan, G. Cheng, B. Li, H. Chen, S. Jin, B. Tan, Controlling Monomer Feeding Rate to Achieve Highly Crystalline Covalent Triazine Frameworks, Adv. Mater., 31 (2019) e1807865.

W. Zhang, L. Chen, S. Dai, C. Zhao, C. Ma, L. Wei, M. Zhu, S.Y. Chong, H. Yang, L. Liu, Y. Bai, M. Yu, Y. Xu, X.W. Zhu, Q. Zhu, S. An, R.S. Sprick, M.A. Little, X. Wu, S. Jiang, Y. Wu, Y.B. Zhang, H. Tian, W.H. Zhu, A.I. Cooper, Reconstructed covalent organic frameworks, Nature, 604 (2022) 72-79.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Yang, F. Kang, X. Wang, Q. Zhang, Design strategies for improving the crystallinity of covalent organic frameworks and conjugated polymers: a review, Mater. Horizons 9(2022) 121-146.
